volume_title: Volume I — The Foundations of Spark
title: Lazy Evaluation & the DAG

blocks.content: Lazy evaluation is the most important concept in Spark. When you call df.filter(), df.select(), or df.join(), Spark does nothing. It just records your intent as a node in a logical plan tree. Computation only starts when you call an action.

rows: select, filter, withColumn, groupBy, join, orderBy, repartition, union
Returns a new DataFrame with added plan node. Zero computation.
Triggers the DAG scheduler. Executes all pending transformations.

blocks.content: Why lazy evaluation? It lets Catalyst see your entire query before executing any of it. Catalyst can then apply global optimizations: push filters down, prune columns early, reorder joins by size. If Spark executed eagerly like Pandas, none of this would be possible.

As you chain transformations, Spark builds a DAG (Directed Acyclic Graph) — a tree of logical operations with your source data at the leaves and the final result at the root. When an action is called, the DAG Scheduler converts this into stages separated at shuffle boundaries.

# Each method call adds a node to the logical DAG:
# Source: read Parquet
df = spark.read.parquet('s3://bucket/events/')
# Node 1: filter (predicate)
df_filtered = df.filter(col('event_type') == 'purchase')
# Node 2: join
df_joined = df_filtered.join(broadcast(df_users), 'user_id')
# Node 3: groupBy+agg
df_agg = df_joined.groupBy('country').agg(sum('amount').alias('total'))
# Node 4: sort
df_sorted = df_agg.orderBy(col('total').desc())

# Nothing has happened yet! DAG is just a plan.
# This triggers execution:
df_sorted.show(20)  # <-- Action

Run df.explain(mode='formatted') BEFORE calling an action to inspect the physical plan Catalyst built. Look for: FileScan (data source), Filter (predicate pushdown), BroadcastHashJoin vs SortMergeJoin, HashAggregate.

The DAG Scheduler cuts the DAG into stages at shuffle boundaries. A shuffle is any operation that requires redistributing data across partitions — groupBy, join (sort-merge), repartition, distinct, orderBy.

rows: Rows with same key must reach same executor
Both tables shuffled by join key
Small table copied to every executor
Range partition shuffle for global sort

blocks.content: The DAG is also Spark's fault tolerance mechanism. If an executor dies mid-job, Spark doesn't restart the whole job. It looks up the lineage of the lost partition and recomputes just those partitions from the last shuffle boundary (or from the source if no shuffle yet).

Deep lineage chains slow down recomputation. If your DAG has 100+ transformations before a shuffle, a single partition failure forces recomputing all 100 steps. Use df.checkpoint() to truncate lineage at key points — it materializes to persistent storage and resets the DAG root.

# Set checkpoint directory (HDFS or S3)
spark.sparkContext.setCheckpointDir('s3://bucket/spark-checkpoints/')

# After a complex, expensive computation:
df_expensive.cache()       # Keep in memory for reuse
df_expensive.checkpoint()  # Also truncate lineage (saves to S3)
df_expensive.count()       # Materialize both cache + checkpoint

rune_cards.definition: Spark transformations don't execute immediately — they build a DAG. Execution starts only when an action is called. Enables Catalyst to see the full query and optimize globally.
Directed Acyclic Graph. Spark's internal representation of your query as a tree of operations. Catalyst converts it from logical → optimized logical → physical plan.
Any operation requiring data redistribution between partitions (groupBy, sort-merge join, orderBy, repartition). Creates a stage boundary in the DAG.
A set of tasks that can run without a shuffle. Each stage = N tasks = N partitions. The DAG Scheduler determines stage boundaries.
df.checkpoint() materializes to HDFS/S3 AND truncates lineage. Unlike cache(), survives executor failures. Use when lineage is 50+ transformations deep.

quiz.question: You write df2 = df.filter(col('x') > 10).select('a','b','c'). When does Spark read from disk?
options: When select() is called, because it's the last transformation
Not yet — no action has been called. Data won't be read until an action like count(), show(), or write()
Spark pre-reads a sample immediately but defers full scan
quiz.explanation: Both filter() and select() are transformations — they add nodes to the logical plan but trigger no I/O. Spark reads data only when you call an action. This is lazy evaluation.

quiz.question: A job has these operations: read → filter → join (sort-merge) → groupBy → orderBy → write. How many shuffle stages does it have?
quiz.explanation: Sort-merge join = 1 shuffle (both tables redistributed by join key). groupBy = 1 shuffle (rows with same key co-located). orderBy = 1 shuffle (range partition for global sort). That's 3 shuffle stages. read and filter have no shuffle. Catalyst may optimize join order, but can't eliminate necessary shuffles.
